# Executive Briefing: Interactive Narrative SaaS
## Generated by CEO synthesis from 4-agent research run

*Run ID: narrative-saas-20260416-004724 • Date: 2026-04-16*

---

### THE OPPORTUNITY IN ONE PARAGRAPH

There is a genuine, sourced structural gap: a 40-books-a-year reader who just finished a series has no next-best-alternative that honors the author's canon, the reader's imagination, and the author's wallet at the same time. Every existing surface fails at least one of those three — fanfic (breaks canon and pays the author nothing), TV/film adaptation (breaks the reader's mental casting), AI Dungeon-class roleplay (breaks canon and makes the author a plaintiff), Kindle Unlimited (preserves canon and pays but doesn't extend the world). The thesis of this business is that a text-only, canon-governed, author-licensed interactive-reading medium can own that whitespace — and that text-only is what makes the unit economics work at a consumer price point book readers will pay. Everything else the research uncovered is either a tailwind, a risk to manage, or a second-order execution problem. The whitespace is real. The question is whether the canon enforcement problem can be solved well enough to honor the author promise at a price readers will accept.

### WHAT IS GENUINELY NEW HERE

Four-way product combination that no living competitor assembles: (1) professionally-authored, licensed story bibles as the supply side, (2) LLM-delivered interactive text as the consumer medium, (3) canon governance that actually enforces author canon against LLM drift and adversarial prompting, (4) ebook-plus-subscription commerce that ties the acquisition funnel directly into the subscription conversion. Character.AI has (2) without any of the others. AI Dungeon has (2) and part of (3) and nearly destroyed itself over content moderation. Kindle Unlimited has (4) without (2) or (3). Choice of Games has (1) and (4) without (2). The novel bet is that executing all four simultaneously is the defensible position — not any single one in isolation.

The subtler novelty is the **inversion of the AI-fiction business model**. Every competitor in the LLM-narrative space treats the AI as the creative center and the author (if present at all) as an afterthought. This business treats the author's bible as the scarce asset and the AI as labor — employed, paid-per-session, with no creative rights of its own. That is a different industry structure, not just a different product. And it is the structure that makes this business *licensable* by publishers and estates, because the value capture diagram tells them exactly what they sell and exactly what they keep.

### THE BUSINESS MODEL

Money flows in three layers, and all three must close for the business to exist.

**Layer 1 — Ebook sale.** Reader buys a text ebook from the platform store. No enhanced media, no audio companion, no graphics — just a text ebook, priced $2.99–$9.99 in Kindle-parity territory. Platform keeps 30%, author/publisher keeps 70%. This mirrors KDP exactly and is non-controversial in contract negotiations with publishers.

**Layer 2 — Subscription (the core product).** Ebook purchase unlocks the interactive world version, gated behind an active subscription. Proposed price: $14.99/month (Audible parity) with a $9.99/month annual-prepaid tier. Subscription revenue is split with author/publisher per world on attributable-session basis. Three structures: established author or major publisher gets 40% sub-share; mid-tier author (the wedge cohort) gets 50%, matching Audible's new exclusive rate; platform-published new author gets 70%, because the platform is already capturing ebook margin on its own imprint.

**Layer 3 — Publishing program.** Platform becomes a publisher for a small new-author cohort each year. Single deal captures two revenue streams: ebook margin at publisher-level economics plus the highest-tier subscription share. For the author this is "Kindle pays you per page, we pay you per page plus a subscription royalty every month the reader returns." For the platform this is the clean-rights supply pipeline that does not require estate diplomacy or established-author agent politics.

**Unit economics at scale (sourced inputs, modeled outputs):**
- Edge-for-80%-of-tokens configuration yields ~$0.07 per session of inference cost, against a reader who does ~20 sessions per month = ~$1.40 per MAU in variable inference.
- At $14.99 ARPU, platform margin after author/publisher sub-share of 40–50%, inference, payment processing, and delivery lands in the $5–$8 per subscriber-month range, before marketing and overhead.
- Ebook-to-subscriber conversion at a realistic 15% and retention at 9 months yields ~$20 LTV per ebook sale. Cold paid-acquisition CAC at Meta median ($38) does not close; ebook-store-as-funnel does.
- **Platform makes ~$60–$90 per active reader per year at steady-state scale**, before author and estate licensing floors are paid from it. Viable at 100K+ MAU; not a rocket-ship consumer business without a clear path past that.

### THE HARDEST PROBLEM

**Defending the canon of a licensed world against an LLM's own drift and a determined user's adversarial prompting — simultaneously — over a multi-hour session, while keeping per-session inference costs below a subscription price that book readers will actually pay.**

This is the FEASIBILITY agent's one-sentence verdict, and both architects' deliverables confirm it. ARCHITECT_2 enumerates ten concrete failure modes (LORE_CONTRADICTION, PHANTOM_ENTITY, UNAUTHORIZED_RULE_INFERENCE, VOICE_COLLAPSE, PREMATURE_REVEAL, FORBIDDEN_ACTION, TONE_BREACH, CONTENT_POLICY_BREACH, TEMPORAL_INCOHERENCE, CANON_AMNESIA), none of which any shipping LLM-narrative product today solves end-to-end. ARCHITECT_1's cost model works only if the edge handles 75–85% of turns — which puts the canon enforcement burden on small on-device models that are precisely the models most prone to the failure modes above.

The architecture is plausible on paper. It has not been built in production. The research does not prove it is solvable at the required quality and the required price — it describes a credible path. That path is the critical-path risk for the company.

### CRITICAL PATH TO VALIDATION

Before writing a line of product code, the next 90 days must answer four questions in order. Each is a hard gate; failing any one should pause the business plan.

1. **Can canon enforcement actually work at small-model scale?** Build a throwaway prototype: take one public-domain story bible (e.g., a Dying Earth homage or an Arthurian cycle), hand-structure it into a World Graph, wire a 3–8B on-device model with hybrid retrieval (pinned hard canon + graph lookup + vector texture) + rule-engine validator, stress-test with adversarial prompting. Target: <5% canon-break rate on a benchmark suite of 200 adversarial turns. If this fails, the entire business fails — everything else is moot.

2. **Will a mid-tier author sign a deal on the proposed rev-share terms?** Run the outreach sequence MARKETING_REVENUE proposed for targets 1–3 (Kobold Press, one mid-tier SFF author via agent, Free League for Vaesen or Coriolis). Goal: one LOI with commercial terms within 60 days. If mid-tier supply doesn't move on the "we pay better than Spotify, canon stays yours" pitch, the platform has no library at launch and the thesis fails.

3. **Does the ebook-to-subscriber conversion reach the 15% center of the sensitivity table?** This cannot be answered without a live prototype, but the research design can: set up a minimal landing page + sample-chapter + first-act-free experience with a waitlist, buy a small targeted paid traffic set to fantasy-reader audiences on Meta and TikTok, measure purchase-to-waitlist-conversion as a leading indicator. Sub-5% conversion kills the unit economics before subscription pricing is even tested.

4. **What is the liability posture, in writing?** Engage external AI-liability counsel and get written positions on: (a) author co-defendant exposure under the Character.AI precedent, (b) LLM provider training-data provenance indemnification (post-Bartz), (c) content moderation policy that threads AI Dungeon's 2021 collapse vs Character.AI's 2024–26 litigation, (d) EU AI Act and Italian DPA Replika-class exposure. If the answer to any of these is "catastrophic and uninsurable", the business does not exist in its current form — and the authors will know before we do.

### WHAT THE ARCHITECTS SAID (AND WHAT IT MEANS FOR THE BUSINESS)

ARCHITECT_1's thesis is that **text-only is the architectural load-bearing decision** — it removes the image, audio, and rendering pipelines that otherwise eat 60–80% of per-session cost and enables the $0.07/session economics that close at an $8–12/month subscription price point. This is not an aesthetic preference dressed up as a business decision. It is a business decision dressed up as an aesthetic preference. The marketing team's "text is the product" framing is therefore not a cope for missing multimodal — it is the positioning of the architectural advantage as a consumer benefit. These two deliverables are aligned and mutually reinforcing, which is good.

ARCHITECT_1's second key call is that **canon is shared read-only memory; reader state is per-session private**. This matters for the business because it is what makes concurrent licensed-IP operation at scale legally coherent — no reader's actions "rewrite" a licensed world for another reader, which is the structural feature estate lawyers and publisher legal departments will actually care about. It is also what lets the platform promise an author that their canon is the product, honestly.

ARCHITECT_2's thesis is that **canon enforcement is a hybrid retrieval + validation problem, not a prompting problem**. Pure vector RAG fails. Pure knowledge graph fails. Pinned hard canon + structured graph + vector texture + rule engine, composed, with narrative-state awareness that tracks what the *reader* knows versus what the world knows, is the only approach that plausibly survives the ten failure modes. The business implication is that **the authoring surface is the real product**. What looks like an author tool (the bible editor, the canon review UI) is the core of the canon engine, and the quality of that tooling is a direct proxy for the quality of canon enforcement the readers will experience. Under-invest in the authoring product and the reader product fails.

ARCHITECT_2's subtler call is that **session-grown canon is a moat**. As readers inhabit a world, the extrapolatable canon gets tested, author-confirmed, and promoted. The world becomes more specified over time. Generic LLM-fiction products cannot replicate this because they have no author in the loop to confirm or reject extrapolation. This is the structural data flywheel the business actually has.

Combined, the two architects are not describing different systems. They are describing the same system from two ends. The interface between them (the `CanonFabric` contract ARCHITECT_1 specifies against ARCHITECT_2's engine) is clean. The architecture is internally consistent.

### MARKET AND COMPETITIVE REALITY

FEASIBILITY's read is that **no competitor assembles all four pillars**, but each pillar's tailwind is real and each pillar's risk is real.

The real competition is not AI Dungeon or Character.AI. The real competition is **reader attention, not reader spend**. The 40-books-a-year reader has a finite number of reading hours; they spend them on Kindle Unlimited ($11.99/month, vast catalog), Audible ($14.99/month, Originals roster), ongoing ebook purchases, library holds, and Substack. The product has to earn a slot in that rotation, and the slot it earns is "I still want to be in this world between books" — not "I want a new kind of entertainment." If the framing drifts to the latter, the product fails the Quibi test: nobody was asking for it.

The regulatory landscape is the scariest surface. Character.AI's Jan 2026 settlement with Google over the Setzer wrongful-death suit, Replika's €5M Italian fine, Anthropic's $1.5B pirated-books settlement, and AI Dungeon's 93% download collapse in 2021 over content moderation are not isolated incidents — they are the post-2024 legal environment for AI narrative products, and this product stacks all of those risk categories on top of each other, with the licensed author now positioned as co-defendant. FEASIBILITY explicitly flags that this alone may prevent established authors from signing. That is why the supply plan leads with TTRPG publishers with clean rights and with a platform-publishing arm for new authors — those are the two supply lanes where the liability math is tractable.

The Tolkien-class estate lane is not a year-one target. The research is clear. Accept it.

### GO-TO-MARKET SEQUENCE

Three moves, in order. Each gates the next.

**Move 1 — Prove the canon engine with a platform-authored demo world (months 0–6).** Before any author signs, the platform writes one world in-house, end-to-end. A tribute-quality, public-domain-adjacent setting (new Arthurian cycle, Dying Earth homage, Gormenghast-adjacent Mervyn Peake pastiche) authored by a small in-house story team explicitly for the purpose. This is both the engineering test harness for canon enforcement *and* the permanent free-tier demo world for reader acquisition. Launching the business without a platform-owned demo world is malpractice — it is the only content the platform controls end-to-end, and it is needed for every subsequent conversation.

**Move 2 — Sign the wedge cohort and launch with five worlds (months 6–12).** Targets 1–5 from the MARKETING_REVENUE playbook: Kobold Press (Midgard), one mid-tier SFF author via agent, Free League (Vaesen or Coriolis), Baen Books author (publisher-level deal), one BookTok indie breakout on the platform-publishing program. Five worlds is the minimum viable catalog — one demo, one TTRPG, one epic fantasy, one genre-adjacent (horror or military-SF), one indie voice. The product launches publicly with these five. Launch channel is ebook-store-as-funnel (not paid ads), seeded through the fandom-specific channels MARKETING_REVENUE named. Target at launch: 5K paying subscribers by month 12, $1M ARR run rate by month 18.

**Move 3 — Expand supply and prove expansion economics (months 12–24).** Targets 6–10, including one marquee signal-booster in long conversation from month 0. This is also when the canon-stewardship bonus program gets tested in earnest — do authors stay engaged, or do they drop the bible and walk? The business model depends on them staying. If by month 24 author retention is below 70% of signed worlds still actively updating canon, the subscription share math changes and the pitch to the next cohort weakens.

Explicitly not in the first 24 months: Tolkien-class estate outreach, sexually explicit content, multi-user shared-world features, video adaptations, audio companion products. These are year-three conversations at the earliest, and only if the canon engine holds.

### OPEN QUESTIONS

- **Reader willingness-to-pay at the 40-books-a-year cohort.** FEASIBILITY flags this as empirically unproven. Character.AI converts ~20M MAU to $2.50 per-user-per-year, and those are AI-natives. Book readers skew older, more AI-skeptical, and more price-anchored to Kindle and Audible. The $14.99 price point may be too aggressive for cold acquisition and too conservative for intensely engaged readers — the pricing ladder is unresolved.
- **Author retention at the canon-stewardship tier.** The model assumes authors stay engaged quarterly. No comparable product exists to benchmark against. Wattpad Paid Stories author retention is a poor analog; Substack's paid-newsletter churn is a better one but still not a direct fit.
- **Whether frontier-model cost decline offsets KV-cache scaling.** ARCHITECT_1's $0.07/session is sensitive to KV-cache behavior at 32K+ context. Silicon Data's observation that doubling context halves concurrent density works against the product's longest sessions — the ones most loyal readers will have. This is a real architectural risk that the 80/20 edge/cloud split mitigates but does not eliminate.
- **Content moderation policy that does not replicate AI Dungeon's 2021 collapse.** The research says "no consensus exists". The business cannot launch without a specific policy, the policy cannot be authored by the platform alone (authors have content preferences that differ world-to-world), and the policy cannot be authored by authors alone (platform-level floors exist for child-safety reasons). The governance model for this is unresolved.
- **How publishers actually respond to the pitch.** The MARKETING_REVENUE outreach playbook is plausible but untested. No publisher has signed this kind of deal before — there is no reference transaction. The first-LOI question is therefore both a commercial question and a format question, and until one is signed, the contract template itself is hypothetical.

### CEO VERDICT

**Conditionally fund.** The whitespace is real, the architecture is plausible, and the author-first business structure is defensible. But three conditions must be met before material capital commits beyond a 90-day validation effort:

1. The canon enforcement prototype (Critical Path question 1) must hit its quality bar on adversarial benchmarks. This is a technical gate, not a business gate. It is also the gate that every other investment depends on.

2. The first LOI (Critical Path question 2) must close on or above the mid-tier rev-share structure. If the platform cannot convert one of the proposed wedge targets at those terms, the thesis that the business "pays better than Spotify" is not actually compelling to the supply side, and the supply model needs a rethink.

3. Written legal positions (Critical Path question 4) must land in the "manageable with specific controls and insurance" zone. Not "unresolved." Not "depends on ongoing litigation." Specific, insurable, and contractable. Without this, the authors will not sign and the insurance market will not underwrite.

**If all three land, this is fundable and I would lead the round.** The market gap is real, the architectural path is credible, the business model is coherent across supply and demand, and the text-only positioning is a durable structural advantage rather than a limitation. The hard problem — canon enforcement at the required quality and price — is the right hard problem to be working on: it is the barrier that keeps the incumbents out, and solving it is the value creation.

**If any of the three fails, do not fund.** Narrative AI platforms that sell themselves on audience scale while skipping the canon and liability problems are heading into the same wall Character.AI and AI Dungeon already hit. The distinguishing bet of this business is that the canon engine is the product. Either the canon engine works at the required quality and cost, or the business is a worse Character.AI. There is no in-between path that is worth a check.
